entry.title: Re: Sales order Header and Item Delivery Block should be...
entry.content:
That is how SAP works. Header Delivery Block will only prevent creation of deliveries and no impact on ATP. Review whether your business requirement is about visibility (order should show incomplete) or logistics (confirmed qty must reset). If visibility is enough, incompletion procedure is sufficient. If logistics alignment is required, clean‑core extensibility is the SAP‑recommended way.
Try to convince the client to go with standard SAP functionality.

entry.title: Offboarding Review Tasks Not Generated – Process Shows Completed
entry.content:
Hi Community,

I have configured the default Offboarding process with:

  • Review Employee Information
  • Review Offboarding Information
  • Offboarding Tasks

Both standard rules are active and return true:

  • ONB2_OFB_ManagerReviewCheck
  • ONB2_OFB_EmployeeReviewCheck

However, after termination, no review task appears for either the manager or employee, and the Offboarding process immediately shows Completed.

The manager assignment, employee user, permissions, and process variant have already been checked.

Has anyone faced this issue or identified any additional configuration that could cause all review steps to be skipped?

entry.title: Introducing Smarter Automation: AI Agent Skills for SAP Automation Pilot
entry.content:
We are excited to announce a new set of AI agent skills for SAP Automation Pilot, now available as an open-source repository on GitHub. Built for Claude Code, these skills bring generative AI directly into your automation development workflow, making it faster and more intuitive to build, review, debug, and manage automations on SAP BTP.

Whether you are just getting started with SAP Automation Pilot or have been working with it for years, these skills remove a significant amount of the manual effort involved in day-to-day automation development. The goal is simple: let you focus on what you want to automate, not on the mechanics of how to express it.

 

What We'll Cover

  • A Brief Introduction to SAP Automation Pilot
  • Where to Find It
  • How It Works
  • A Closer Look at Each Skill
  • Prerequisites and Setup
  • A Sample Prompt to Get You Started
  • Start Building Today
  • Useful Links

 

A Brief Introduction to SAP Automation Pilot

SAP Automation Pilot is SAP's built-in automation service for SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP). It is built for DevOps and Operations teams who need to automate complex operational tasks - from application and database lifecycle management to incident response, health monitoring, and scheduled workflows, without writing extensive custom code.

If you are not familiar with SAP Automation Pilot yet, the official documentation is a great place to start: SAP Automation Pilot Documentation

 

Where to Find It

The full repository is publicly available on GitHub:

  • SAP Automation Pilot Agent Skills - GitHub

All ten skills are included and ready to use. The repository is open source and free to clone.

 
 

How It Works

Once set up, these skills are available directly inside Claude Code - whether you use the VS Code extension, the CLI, or the desktop app. From there, you interact with SAP Automation Pilot through natural conversation rather than manual configuration.

The skills are designed to support you across the entire automation lifecycle – building new commands, reviewing and validating your work, deploying content to your tenant, triggering and monitoring executions, investigating failures, managing schedules, and more. Rather than covering one specific area, they work together as a complete toolkit for SAP Automation Pilot development and operations.

 
 

A Closer Look at Each Skill

  • Automation Pilot Command Generation – Generate production-ready commands from a plain language description, with error handling and best practices built in.
  • Automation Pilot Catalog Explorer – Browse and search existing commands and catalogs in your tenant before building something new.
  • Automation Pilot Command Review – Validates commands, inputs, and catalogs against security requirements, naming conventions, and best practices.
  • Automation Pilot Content Management via API – Create, update, list, and remove commands and catalogs in your tenant through the Content API.
  • Automation Pilot Executions API – Trigger commands, monitor progress, retrieve logs, and control executions programmatically.
  • Automation Pilot Debugger – Pulls logs, filters failures, and matches error patterns to remediation suggestions when an execution goes wrong.
  • Automation Pilot Scheduled Executions API – Create and manage recurring automation schedules with flexible timing and full timezone support.
  • Automation Pilot MCP Server Generation – Expose your SAP Automation Pilot commands as tools for AI assistants via MCP server definitions.
  • Automation Pilot HTTP Request Executor – Guidance for configuring the HTTP executor used in most API-facing commands.
  • Automation Pilot ExecuteScript Executor – Covers script executors for Bash, Python, Node.js, and PowerShell with language-specific best practices.

 

Prerequisites and Setup

Prerequisites:

  • Claude Code (VS Code extension, CLI, or desktop app)
  • curl and jq installed on your machine
  • An SAP Automation Pilot tenant with API credentials

Setup:

  1. Clone the repository from GitHub
  2. Create a .env file with your connection details — your regional AUTOPI_HOSTNAME (emea, amer, apac, aus, or ksa), AUTOPI_USERNAME, AUTOPI_PASSWORD, and AUTOPI_DEFAULT_CATALOG
  3. Run source .env to load your environment
  4. Open Claude Code and start a conversation

 

A Sample Prompt to Get You Started

Here is an example of what you can do with the Command Generation skill right away:

"Create an SAP Automation Pilot Command that restarts a Cloud Foundry app and waits for it to become healthy, with proper error handling."

From that single prompt, Claude Code will generate a complete, production-ready SAP Automation Pilot command – correctly structured, with health check logic, error handling, and best practices already in place.

 

Start Building Today

Getting started takes only a few minutes, and the skills are ready to use from your very first conversation. Try it with your own SAP Automation Pilot tenant and see how much faster your automation development can move.

 

Useful Links

  • SAP Automation Pilot Agent Skills - GitHub
  • AI Skills Library
  • SAP Automation Pilot Documentation
  • Claude Code Documentation

entry.title: Stop Guessing. Start Simulating. The Case for Data-Driven Business Process Simulation
entry.content:
Every business transformation starts with a simple question:  what will happen if we make this change?

It's a question that organizations spend millions trying to answer. Teams build business cases, analyze KPIs, consult experts, and benchmark against peers. Yet despite all the planning, transformation initiatives often deliver unexpected results. A bottleneck shifts instead of disappearing. An automation project accelerates one activity while slowing down another. A redesign that looked perfect in workshops creates unforeseen issues once it reaches production.

The challenge isn't a lack of ideas, it's a lack of certainty.

 

From Opinion to Evidence

Traditional process simulation has existed for decades, and has been consistently underused for a simple reason: it required an enormous amount of manual work before it could produce a single meaningful result. Analysts had to build process models from scratch, estimate parameters by hand, make simplifying assumptions about resources and timing, and then hope their guesses were close enough to reality to make the output trustworthy. In most organisations, the effort required to set up a simulation exceeded the value it returned.

The new Data-driven business process simulation capability, released in beta in July 28th, changes this equation.

By combining real process execution data with advanced simulation capabilities, organizations can test change scenarios before implementation, evaluate trade-offs, and quantify business impact in a risk-free environment. Instead of making decisions based on assumptions, intuition, or incomplete information, teams can validate improvements using evidence grounded in how their processes actually operate.

Two Ways In, One Source of Truth

SAP Signavio Process Intelligence offers two entry points to data-driven business process simulation, designed to meet organisations wherever they are.

For teams with access to event log data, a fully data-driven simulation is available immediately. The engine reads the historical execution record, learns the core simulation parameters automatically, and produces a working model of how your process behaves, without requiring any manual configuration. This is a statistically grounded representation of how your process has actually been running, ready to be the baseline against which you test every proposed change.

For teams with established process models but limited execution data, the model-based entry point provides a credible simulation capability without requiring an event log. This path also replaces legacy simulation tools that relied on manual parameterisation, giving organisations a modern, maintainable simulation foundation built on the same platform as their process governance and analytics.

Both paths converge at the same place: a scenario management layer that lets you create, execute, and compare simulation scenarios via APIs, generating synthetic event logs that represent proposed future states and enabling direct comparison of as-is versus to-be KPIs.

What This Makes Possible

  • When you are considering automating an activity, simulation tells you whether the process is stable enough to automate and what the downstream impact on cycle time and resource utilisation will be, before a single bot is deployed.
  • When you are evaluating whether to add headcount to a struggling team, simulation tells you whether the bottleneck is actually a capacity problem or a routing problem, before the hiring decision is made.
  • When you are building a business case for a process redesign, simulation provides the quantified, evidence-based projections that move a conversation from opinion to decision, before the investment is committed.

This is the shift from reactive to predictive process management. Instead of implementing a change and measuring the consequences, you test the consequences before the implementation. Instead of discovering bottlenecks after go-live, you identify them in a safe environment where they cost nothing to fix. Instead of aligning stakeholders on assumptions, you align them on evidence.

The Foundation for Intelligent Process Transformation

Data-driven business process simulation does not sit alongside your process intelligence capabilities, it amplifies them. Conformance checking tells you where your process is deviating today. Root cause analysis tells you why. Simulation tells you what will happen if you fix it, and which fix produces the best outcome across cycle time, cost, and resource utilisation simultaneously.

Together, these capabilities form a closed loop: discover how processes actually run, understand why they perform the way they do, simulate the impact of changing them, implement with confidence, and monitor the result continuously. Each step in the loop is grounded in the same operational data. Each decision is supported by the same evidence base.

For organisations that have invested in process modeling, data-driven simulation completes the picture. Your designed process becomes the benchmark. Your event data becomes the calibration. And your proposed improvements get tested in a virtual environment where failure is free, before you commit them to the real one.

The organisations that transform fastest are not the ones that move quickest.

They are the ones that know what they are doing before they do it.

entry.title: MPR Score section blank for specific employee in Perf form (SF)
entry.content:
We are facing an issue in our Performance Management form (template: "Average MPR Score : BPO") where the "Average MPR Score" section renders completely empty for a specific employee, while the same section displays correctly (with the rating dropdown and item "1.1 Average MPR Score") for other employees on the exact same route map and template.

Details:

  • Form: BPO-Performance Appraisal Review 2025-26 (Supervisory_MPR)
  • Affected employee: shows "Average MPR Score (80.0%)" and "Competency (20.0%)" headers, but no rating item/dropdown appears underneath the Average MPR Score heading
  • Competency section items and ratings display and function normally
  • Route Map is identical for both affected and unaffected employees
  • Field "Average MPR Score" (Field ID: bizx-actual, Type: number) settings look consistent across the template
  • Checked Field Permissions under Edit Categories — found a Write permission block scoped to Role: "HR Rep." for Route Step "Average MPR Score"

Any guidance on where to check next (Goal Plan data, Manage Form Goal Plan Data Association, or dynamic role resolution) would be appreciated.

entry.title: MCP Servers on SAP Integration Suite : Build Your First MCP Server from an API
entry.content:
Introduction

In the previous blog, I introduced the Model Context Protocol (MCP) and explored how it provides a standardized way for AI applications and agents to discover and invoke enterprise capabilities. We also saw that MCP does not replace APIs—it complements them by exposing existing business services in an AI-friendly, discoverable format.

In this article, we move from concept to implementation by creating our first MCP Server on SAP Integration Suite. Using an managed API as source, we will expose selected business operations as AI-ready tools, deploy the MCP Server on Integration Cell, and finally invoke it from an AI agent using Cline. Along the way, we will also see how MCP Servers help enterprises securely expose governed business capabilities to AI agents while leveraging existing API investments.

MCP Server on SAP Integration Suite

Before we begin the implementation, it is useful to briefly revisit the role of an MCP Server in an enterprise architecture. An MCP Server exposes enterprise capabilities as AI-ready tools that can be discovered and invoked by MCP-compatible applications. In SAP Integration Suite, MCP Servers can be created from APIs, HTTP Endpoints, or SAP RFCs, enabling organizations to securely expose existing business capabilities to AI agents without changing the underlying backend systems.

This blog focuses on creating an MCP Server from an API artifact. The HTTP Endpoint and RFC options will be covered in subsequent articles in this series.

For readers who are new to MCP or would like a deeper understanding of concepts such as MCP Server, Tools, Resources, Prompts, MCP Client, and MCP Host, kindly refer to the first article in this series: Model Context Protocol (MCP): From APIs to AI-Ready Interfaces

Scenario

Imagine a procurement manager preparing for the daily purchasing review meeting. Before the meeting begins, they need to quickly understand the current state of several purchase orders without navigating multiple SAP applications or manually searching through different screens. Instead of opening SAP S/4HANA and searching for each purchase order individually, the manager simply asks an AI assistant:

"Show me Purchase Order 4199999998 along with its line items and schedule lines."

To answer this request, the AI assistant must retrieve information from multiple business entities exposed by the SAP S/4HANA Purchase Order API. Rather than knowing which APIs or OData entities to call, the assistant discovers the appropriate MCP Tools, invokes them through the MCP Server, and combines the results into a single, easy-to-understand response.

In this blog, we will create an MCP Server on SAP Integration Suite that exposes selected read-only Purchase Order operations as MCP Tools. We will then deploy the MCP Server, publish it through Developer Hub, and demonstrate how an AI agent can discover and invoke these tools using natural language.

What We Are Building

We will expose selected Purchase Order operations from an SAP S/4HANA managed API as MCP Tools. The MCP Server will be deployed on Integration Cell and consumed by an AI agent through Cline. Natural language requests are translated into MCP Tool invocations, which execute the managed API and return business data from SAP S/4HANA.

The end-to-end flow :

                                                                                  

 

Prerequisites

Before you begin, ensure that the following prerequisites are met:

  • Supported Service Plan: The MCP Server artifact is available only with supported SAP Integration Suite service plans, including Enhanced, Premium, Trial, and Free Tier. For the latest availability details, refer to SAP Note 2903776.
  • SAP Integration Suite: Your SAP Integration Suite tenant is provisioned and the API Management capability is activated.
  • Integration Cell Runtime: An Integration Cell runtime is activated and available. If you haven't configured Integration Cell yet, refer to my previous blog (section : Activate the Integration Cell Runtime)
  • Developer Role: The PI_Integration_Developer role collection is assigned to your user to create, configure, and deploy MCP Server artifacts.

Model and Deploy the MCP Server

With the prerequisites in place, we can now create our first MCP Server on SAP Integration Suite. Navigate to your package under Design → Integrations and APIs → Artifacts, click Add, and select MCP Server.

 

 Step 1: Select the Source Type

The first step in the wizard is to choose the source from which the MCP Server will be created. SAP Integration Suite currently supports three source types:

  • API – Create an MCP Server from an existing API artifact deployed on Integration Cell.
  • HTTP Endpoint with OpenAPI Specification – Create an MCP Server by importing an OpenAPI specification and exposing operations from any HTTP endpoint.
  • RFC – Create an MCP Server from SAP RFC-enabled function modules by exposing selected RFC operations as MCP Tools.

In this blog, we will use the API option. Our source API is an SAP S/4HANA Purchase Order API that has already been created and deployed on Integration Cell. If you would like to learn how to create and deploy a managed API on Integration Suite, refer to my previous blog.

 

Step 2: Provide MCP Server Details

Next, provide the basic information required to create the MCP Server

 

Step 3: Select the Source API

Click the value help beside the API field to open the API selection dialog. The dialog lists all eligible API artifacts that have been deployed on Integration Cell and can be used as the source for an MCP Server. It will list OData and REST APIs deployed on Integration Cell. For this example, select the SAP S/4HANA Purchase Order API. Once selected, the wizard automatically associates the MCP Server with the API and imports its OpenAPI specification for tool generation.

Step 4: Select the MCP Tools

After selecting the source API, the wizard displays all operations defined in the API specification. Each operation can be exposed as an MCP Tool. Although every operation can technically be selected, it is recommended to expose only those business capabilities that are meaningful for AI agents. Avoid exposing internal or administrative operations that are not intended for conversational use.

After selecting the required operations, click Add to generate the MCP Tools.

Configure the MCP Server

Once the MCP Server has been created, you can further configure and customize it from the MCP Configuration tab before deploying it. The configuration is organized into four sections: Source, Tools, Resources, and Prompts.

Source

The Source tab displays the enterprise asset from which the MCP Server was created. In this example, the source is the managed SAP S/4HANA Purchase Order API.

The source association is read-only and provides traceability between the MCP Server and the underlying API. If the source API evolves over time, the MCP Server can be synchronized to incorporate the latest changes.

 

Tools

The Tools tab lists all MCP Tools exposed by the server. Each tool represents a business capability derived from an API operation.

Selecting a tool displays its configuration, including:

  •   Tool name, title, and description – These fields are editable and should clearly describe the business capability exposed by the tool. Since AI agents and MCP clients rely on this metadata to discover and select the appropriate tool, use descriptive, action-oriented names and concise descriptions that explain what the tool does, when it should be used, and what information it returns. Well-defined metadata improves the agent's ability to make accurate tool selection decisions.
  •   HTTP method and API resource path – Identifies the underlying API operation invoked by the tool.
  •   Generated input schema – Defines the parameters that the AI application must provide when invoking the tool.
  •   Generated output schema – Describes the structure of the response returned by the tool.
  •   API Reference – Provides a direct link to the corresponding API operation for additional implementation details.

The generated schemas define the contract between the AI application and the MCP Server, enabling MCP-compatible clients to understand the expected inputs and outputs without requiring knowledge of the underlying API. The Add action allows additional API operations to be exposed as MCP Tools after the MCP Server has been created, making it easy to extend the server as new business capabilities become available.

Resources

Resources are intended for contextual information rather than executable business actions. Typical examples include documentation, reference data, configuration information, or knowledge content that AI applications can retrieve during reasoning.

The Add action can be used to define and expose additional Resources that complement the executable Tools exposed by the MCP Server.

Prompts

The Prompts tab enables reusable prompt templates to be defined and exposed by the MCP Server. These prompts provide standardized instructions that AI applications can leverage for common tasks, ensuring consistent interactions across different MCP clients.

Step 1: Define Prompt

Provide a unique Name, a meaningful Title, and a clear Description for the prompt. Use descriptive names and concise descriptions so AI agents and MCP clients can easily understand the prompt's purpose and determine when it should be used.

Step 2: Define Arguments

Arguments define the input parameters that an AI application shall provide when invoking the prompt. Design arguments to capture only the business information required by the prompt, making them intuitive and easy for both users and AI agents to supply.

Step 3: Define Messages

Messages define the conversational instructions that guide the AI model. Typically, a Assistant Message establishes the assistant's role and expected behaviour, while the User Message captures the task to be performed, using the defined arguments where applicable.

 

Synchronize API Changes to Your MCP Server

As your backend APIs evolve, the corresponding MCP Server should also reflect those changes. If the source API artifact is updated—for example by adding or removing API resources, modifying request or response schemas, or changing authentication settings—you can use the Synchronize action to align the MCP Server with the latest API definition. This helps ensure that AI agents continue to discover and invoke accurate tools and resources without manual reconfiguration.

Note: The Synchronize option is available only for MCP Servers created from an API as source.

During synchronization, Integration Suite detects changes between the source API and the MCP Server and presents them for review before applying the updates.

Category

Description

Tools

Detects newly added API resources, removed API resources, and changes to the input or output schema of existing MCP Tools.

Resources

Detects MCP Resources whose corresponding API resources have been removed from the source API.

Authentication Policy Changes

Detects updates to the source API authentication policy that may affect how the MCP Server authenticates requests to the backend.

Schema Changes

Detects modifications to request or response schemas of API resources already exposed as MCP Tools or Resources, ensuring AI agents receive the latest contract.

After reviewing the detected changes, you can apply the synchronization to keep your MCP Server fully aligned with the current API specification. This minimizes maintenance effort while ensuring AI agents always interact with the latest enterprise capabilities. 

Policies

An MCP Server is more than a collection of AI tools—it is an enterprise endpoint that must be secured and governed. To achieve this, every MCP Server created in SAP Integration Suite is provisioned with a default policy model that can be further customized based on your organization's security and governance requirements.

By default, the policy model includes the MCP Sender Adapter, HTTP Receiver Adapter, Authentication, and Authorization policies. Together, these components provide a secure communication channel between the AI application and the backend business system.

 

MCP Adapter (Sender)

The MCP Adapter acts as the entry point for MCP-compatible clients. It receives requests from AI applications using the Model Context Protocol and forwards them into the policy processing pipeline.

Authentication Policy

When an MCP Server is created from an API, the Authentication policy is automatically inherited from the source API. This ensures that the MCP Server follows the same authentication mechanism as the underlying API, providing a consistent security model without requiring additional configuration.

Since the authentication settings are inherited, they are read-only within the MCP Server and cannot be modified independently. Any changes to the authentication mechanism should be made on the source API, after which the MCP Server can be synchronized to inherit the updated configuration.

 

Authorization Policy

The Authorization policy determines whether an authenticated client is permitted to invoke the MCP Server. In this example, the authorization type is set to OAuth Scope or Developer Key, allowing access to be controlled either through OAuth scopes or API consumer credentials.

The default required scope is API.invoke. When provisioning the Process Integration Runtime instance, administrators must assign the API.invoke scope so that authenticated MCP clients are authorized to invoke the exposed tools. We will cover this configuration later in the blog while creating the Process Integration Runtime instance.

Note: If the source API is already protected by an Authorization policy, enable the Trust Upstream MCP Authorization option in the source API authorization policy. When selected, the MCP Server trusts the authorization decision made by the upstream API and does not perform an additional authorization check, preventing duplicate policy evaluation and ensuring a single, consistent authorization model across the API and the MCP Server.

 

HTTP Adapter (Receiver)

The HTTP Adapter connects the MCP Server to the underlying managed API. Rather than requiring its own endpoint configuration, it automatically references the source API selected during MCP Server creation, ensuring that all requests are routed through the managed API.

 

 

Once the configuration is complete, deploy the MCP Server. Deployment provisions the runtime endpoint on Integration Cell, making the configured MCP Tools available for discovery and invocation by MCP-compatible AI applications.

 

 

 

Credential to access MCP Server

An MCP Server can be invoked using credentials obtained through either a Process Integration Runtime (PIR) service instance or Developer Hub subscription credentials. In this blog, we will use the Process Integration Runtime approach. The Developer Hub subscription flow will be covered in the next article in this series.

To invoke the deployed MCP Server from an external AI application such as Cline, create a Process Integration Runtime service instance. The generated Service Key contains the OAuth client credentials required to obtain an access token and securely invoke the MCP Server endpoint.

Step 1: Create a Process Integration Runtime Instance

In your SAP BTP subaccount, navigate to Instances and Subscriptions and create a new SAP Process Integration Runtime service instance.

Step 2: Configure Roles

During instance creation, select the required OAuth grant type (Client Credentials) and assign the API.invoke role. The API.invoke role is required because the deployed MCP Server is protected by the Authorization policy configured earlier. Without this role, authenticated clients will not be authorized to invoke the MCP Tools.

Step 3: Create a Service Key

After the service instance has been created, create a Service Key for the instance. The generated service key contains the OAuth client credentials, including the Client ID, Client Secret, Token URL, and other connection details required by MCP-compatible clients. These credentials will be used in the next section to configure Cline and securely connect to the deployed MCP Server.

 

Invoke the MCP Server Using an AI Client

To demonstrate the end-to-end flow, I used Visual Studio Code with the Cline extension as the MCP-compatible AI client. Cline is connected to the deployed MCP Server using the Process Integration Runtime credentials generated in the previous section. The detailed steps for configuring Cline are outside the scope of this blog.

Once the connection is established, Cline automatically discovers all capabilities exposed by the MCP Server. The available Tools, Resources, and Prompts are retrieved dynamically through the Model Context Protocol, eliminating the need for any manual tool registration or custom integration.

As shown below, Cline has discovered the Purchase Order MCP Server and its exposed capabilities. The AI client can inspect each tool, understand its description, required parameters, and available prompts before deciding which tool to invoke for a user's request. This dynamic discovery is one of the key advantages of MCP over traditional API integrations.

This naturally leads into your final section where you ask Cline:

"Show me Purchase Order 4199999998 along with its line items and schedule lines."

and then show how the LLM automatically selects and invokes the appropriate MCP tools. This provides a compelling end-to-end demonstration of the complete flow.

With the MCP Server connected, the AI application can now invoke enterprise capabilities using natural language. In this example, the user asks Cline to retrieve a Purchase Order along with its line items and schedule lines, without specifying any API endpoints or implementation details.

Cline analyzes the request, discovers the appropriate MCP Tools exposed by the Purchase Order MCP Server, and automatically determines the sequence of tool invocations required to fulfill the request. It invokes the relevant tools, retrieves data from SAP S/4HANA through the MCP Server, and consolidates the responses into a single business-friendly summary.

This demonstrates one of the key advantages of MCP: AI agents reason about the user's intent and dynamically select the appropriate enterprise tools, eliminating the need for developers to hardcode API calls or orchestration logic.

Conclusion

In this blog, we walked through the complete lifecycle of creating an MCP Server on SAP Integration Suite using an existing Managed API. Starting with an SAP S/4HANA Purchase Order API, we created and deployed an MCP Server, explored its configuration and policy model, generated secure runtime credentials, connected an AI client, and finally invoked enterprise business capabilities using natural language.

This demonstrates how SAP Integration Suite enables organizations to transform existing APIs into AI-ready, governed enterprise tools without modifying backend applications. By combining MCP with the security, governance, and lifecycle management capabilities of API Management, enterprise APIs become readily consumable by any MCP-compatible AI application.

In the next blog of this series, we will explore how to create an MCP Server from an HTTP Endpoint by importing an OpenAPI Specification (OAS). This approach enables organizations to expose any standards-based HTTP service as an MCP Server, even when it is not managed as an API artifact in SAP Integration Suite. A subsequent blog will cover creating MCP Servers from SAP RFCs, extending AI access to native ABAP business logic.

entry.title: SAP Digital Mfg: "Create HU" button missing in GR dialog in Process Order POD 2.0 with HU Mgmt.
entry.content:
In SAP Digital Manufacturing, when posting a goods receipt for a Process Order using a Process Order POD (POD 2.0), the goods receipt dialog does not display a "Create HU" button alongside the Handling Unit field, allowing the operator to generate the next HU number automatically (as was available in POD 1.0).

The Process Order POD was created using Manage PODs 2.0 app and is a copy of the standard 'Order POD' template. The standard Goods Receipt Table plugin is used as-is from the template.

Is there a way to enable this? I could not find any plugin properties related to HU number generation.

Confirmed working:

  • Manage Next Numbers: HU number range is correctly configured in SAP DM.
  • EWM number range for HU identification is correctly defined.
  • EWM-managed storage location is correctly configured in Manage Storage Locations.
  • HU numbers can be created and are accepted in EWM if using a POD based on POD 1.0

entry.title: SAP Integration with Concur Solutions: Substitution Rules
entry.content:
This blog post highlights the newest enhancements to substitution rules in SAP Integration with Concur Solutions (also referred to as ICS or Native Integration) delivered with SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud 2608. For example, you can now use the Concur Substitution Rules app to define rules for journal entry header fields. To make things practical, I’ll also walk you through an example of configuring Concur-specific substitution rules.

What’s new in the Manage Concur Substitution Rules app?

  • Additional source fields are now available for defining substitution rules. In fact, nearly every non-technical field from an expense report JSON is now available.
  • Additional target fields are available for defining substitution rules at the journal entry line item level.
  • Substitution rules can now be defined at the journal entry header level as well.

The graphic below shows the list of fields which are available at the line item level:

Target Fields at Line Item Level

 

The following fields are available at the header level:

Target Fields at Header Level

 

 

 

Example Scenario

Let’s assume the following requirements:

  • My organization wants to adjust the journal entry header text and the document reference ID to indicate that the journal entry was created for the CEO’s expense report.
  • My organization wants to generate statistical postings on the traveler’s home cost center whenever travel costs are not allocated to a cost center.

To meet these requirements, I need to configure two substitution rules in the Manage Concur Substitution Rules app.

Rule 1: Adjusting the journal entry header

I create a rule for the business context "Expense Report – Header Level". As a precondition, I check whether the traveler’s employee ID matches the CEO’s employee ID. I then define the following substitutions:

  • The AccountingDocumentHeaderText should be substituted with the constant value "CEO’s Travel Expenses".
  • The DocumentReferenceID should be substituted with a concatenation of the expense report number and the constant value "CEO", separated by a slash (/).

The graphic illustrates how to configure this rule:

Rule for Adjusting the Journal Entry Header

 

 

Rule 2: Adjusting the journal entry line items

Next, I create a rule for the business context "Expense Report – Line Item Level" with two preconditions:

  1. The rule should only apply when the expense isn’t allocated to a cost center. Since an expense can be split across multiple cost objects—each generating a separate journal entry line item—I need to evaluate this at the allocation level. In my setup, this information is stored in CUSTOM3 on the expense entry allocation level.
  2. The rule should only apply to expense G/L account line items. ICS adds cost object information only to expense G/L account line items, and since I expect only debit postings on these accounts, I restrict the rule to item type EXP (Expense Account Debit).

In my organization’s setup, the employee’s home cost center is stored in CUSTOM13 of the employee master data record within Concur. This value is available during expense report posting via the source field EmployeeCustom13Code. Therefore, I define that EmployeeCustom13Code should substitute the content of the CostCenter field.

The graphic illustrates how to configure this rule:

Rule for Adjusting the Journal Entry Line Items

 

 

Result: What Does the Journal Entry Look Like?

Let’s take a look at the CEO’s expense report, which contains one expense whose allocation is split evenly between a cost center and a WBS element. With the new substitution rules applied, you can observe the following changes in the resulting journal entry and compare them to a standard ICS posting:

Journal Entry Header

 

Journal Entry Line Item

 

Feel free to leave a comment if you have questions or want to share feedback!

entry.title: Work Smarter with AI-Assisted Tasks in the SAP Activate Project Management Workstream
entry.content:
This blog post is part of a series highlighting AI capabilities in SAP Activate. For additional information, visit the main blog here. 

Project Managers are expected to balance strategic planning with day-to-day execution while keeping projects on schedule, within budget, and aligned with business objectives. To help streamline these activities, the SAP Activate road map viewer now includes AI-assisted procedures for tasks within the Project Management workstream.

These AI-Assisted procedures are designed to help project managers quickly create high-quality project deliverables by combining SAP Activate best practices with generative AI. Rather than starting from a blank page, project teams can leverage guided AI prompts to accelerate planning, improve consistency, and reduce manual effort.

What Are AI-Assisted Procedures?

AI-Assisted procedures provide step-by-step guidance for using generative AI to complete common project management activities. Each procedure includes a structured prompt that incorporates SAP Activate methodology and best practices, helping generate a tailored starting point that can be reviewed and refined for your specific project.

The generated content is intended to support project teams by providing a solid first draft while allowing project managers to apply their expertise and project-specific knowledge before finalizing the deliverable.

The following Project Management tasks now include AI-Assisted procedures with the SAP Activate road map viewer:

Develop a Project Charter

A well-defined project charter establishes the project’s purpose, objectives, scope, governance, and success criteria. The AI-Assisted procedure helps generate a comprehensive project charter by incorporating information such as project goals, stakeholders, timelines, assumptions, constraints, risks, and governance structure.

This provides project managers with a structured draft that can be customized to meet the organization's specific requirements.

Estimate Backlog Items

Accurate backlog estimation is essential for planning releases and managing project delivery. The AI-Assisted procedure assists teams in developing consistent effort estimates by analyzing backlog item descriptions and applying estimation guidance based on SAP Activate best practices.

The resulting estimates can be reviewed and refined by the project team during backlog and sprint planning activities.

Develop a Hypercare Support Plan

Preparing for a post-go-live support is critical to ensuring a successful transition into operations. The AI-Assisted procedure helps generate a structured Hypercare Support plan that outlines support objectives, roles and responsibilities, issue management processes, communication plans, monitoring activities, escalation paths, and exit criteria.

This provides implementation teams with a comprehensive starting point for planning the Hypercare period.

Handover Operations Responsibility

A smooth transition from the implementation project to the operational support organization is key to long-term success. The AI-Assisted procedure assists project teams in developing an operational handover plan that defines transition activities, knowledge transfer, documentation requirements, support responsibilities, and operational readiness.

The generated plan helps ensure that operational teams receive the information they need to effectively support the solution after go-live.

Communicate Project Status and Progress

Keeping stakeholders informed with timely, accurate, and consistent project updates is essential for effective project governance. The AI-Assisted procedure helps project managers create a comprehensive project status report by generating a structured summary of overall project health, milestone progress, schedule and budget status, key accomplishments, risks and issues, upcoming activities, and decisions requiring stakeholder attention.

This provides project managers with a professional, SAP Activate-aligned starting point that can be reviewed, tailored with project specific information, and shared with stakeholders to support informed decision making and transparent project communication.

Why Use AI-Assisted Procedures?

By incorporating AI-Assisted procedures into the SAP Activate methodology project teams can:

  • Accelerate the creation of common project management deliverables.
  • Start with structured, SAP Activate-aligned content instead of a blank document.
  • Improve consistency across projects by leveraging standardized guidance.
  • Reduce the time spent drafting project documentation.
  • Allow project managers to focus more on project leadership, stakeholder engagement, and decision-making.

These procedures are designed to enhance, not replace, the expertise of experienced project managers. Human review remains an essential part of the process to validate accuracy, tailor the content to the project, and ensure alignment with organizational standards.

The AI-Assisted Procedure tag helps you quickly identify tasks that include guidance for using generative AI. These tasks contain an AI-Assisted procedure and a link to the SAP Activate Prompt Library, making it easy to generate first drafts of project deliverables while following SAP Activate best practices.

entry.title: Re: Unable to Set Payment Terms and Payment Method for Ve...
entry.content:
Dear @StrOk,

Thank you for the additional information. I reproduced the scenario in Public Cloud using Clear Outgoing Payments (F1367). The tests confirmed that both Payment Term from Invoice and Fixed Payment Term in SSCUI 102923 can be applied to a supplier residual item created during customer/supplier cross-clearing

Test 1 – Payment Term from Invoice

The blank/default tolerance group was used with:

  • Payment Term from Invoice selected
  • Fixed Payment Term left blank

The original supplier item contained:

  • Payment Terms: NT15
  • Baseline Date: 22.05.2025
  • Net Due Date: 06.06.2025
  • Payment Method: A

The clearing was performed with:

1. Transfer Posting = ON

2. Journal Entry Type = ZV – Payment Clearing

3. Supplier and customer items selected using Select More

4. Residual Amount entered on the supplier invoice line

5. A reason code without Write Off Difference (SSCUI 103106 – Define Difference Reasons for Payment Differences)

The resulting supplier residual item inherited Payment Terms NT15, Baseline Date 22.05.2025, Net Due Date 06.06.2025 and Payment Method A. The values were visible in the simulation the posted journal entry and Manage Supplier Line Items

Test 2 – Fixed Payment Term

For the second test, I created a dedicated tolerance group to remove any ambiguity in tolerance group determination. The supplier was assigned to this group with:

  • Payment Term from Invoice not selected
  • Fixed Payment Term = 0004

The original supplier invoice had Payment Terms 0002 and a blank Payment Method. The same F1367 cross-clearing procedure was then repeated

The resulting supplier residual item contained:

  • Payment Terms: 0004
  • Baseline Date: 31.07.2026
  • Net Due Date: 31.07.2026
  • Payment Method: blank

This confirms that Fixed Payment Term 0004 replaced Payment Terms 0002 from the original supplier invoice

The Payment Method is not controlled by the Fixed Payment Term setting. In the first test, Payment Method A was inherited because it existed on the original supplier line item. In the second test, it remained blank because it was also blank on the original supplier line item

Based on these results, please verify the following in your scenario:

  1. The intended tolerance group is determined for the supplier in the relevant company code
  2. The original supplier line item not only the supplier master data contains the expected Payment Terms and Payment Method
  3. Transfer Posting is enabled in F1367
  4. Journal Entry Type ZV – Payment Clearing is used. SAP documents ZV as the journal entry type for customer/supplier cross-clearing
  5. The residual amount is entered on the supplier invoice line
  6. The selected reason code does not have Write Off Difference enabled
  7. Any automatically proposed cash discount is removed if it prevents the document from balancing
  8. The payment data is checked in both the simulation and the posted residual item

I did not identify an additional configuration beyond SSCUI 102923 and the correct F1367 execution. Therefore, if the same settings and procedure are used but the Payment Terms are still blank, an SAP incident should be raised with the tolerance-group configuration, source supplier item, F1367 header, residual-item details, simulation and posted journal entry

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: Automatic Printing of PM Orders Generated from Maintenance Plans in S4 Hana Public Cloud
entry.content:
We have a requirement in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition (2SL) to automatically generate and print the Job Card whenever a PM Maintenance Order is created from a Maintenance Plan.

We are aware that Job Card outputs can be generated using the Schedule Output for Maintenance Orders app. However, it is not clear whether this process can be fully automated for maintenance orders created through maintenance plan scheduling.

Could you please advise:

  • Is there a standard way to automatically trigger Job Card output when a Maintenance Order is generated from a Maintenance Plan?
  • Can Output Parameter Determination (OPD) or Output Management be configured to achieve this without manual intervention?
  • If standard functionality is not available, are there any recommended approaches using background jobs, or extensibility options?

Our goal is to avoid users having to manually select and send output items and instead have the Job Card generated automatically as soon as the Maintenance Order is created.

 

Thanks in advance.

entry.title: Re: Building a Customer Master Data Input Form Using SAC Scripting
entry.content:
Hi @Ganesh03 

Yes, bulk status updates are possible — here's my approach:

Step 1: Add a radio button to select the status scope
Create a radio button with two options, Active and Inactive. This lets the user choose which group of customers they want to work with.

Step 2: Add a multi-select checkbox list of customers
Based on the radio button selection, populate a checkbox widget with the corresponding customers (e.g., if "Active" is selected, show only active customers). The user can then tick the checkboxes for all the customers whose status they want to change.

Step 3: Add a button to apply the update
On clicking this button, the status of all selected customers is updated in one go (from Active to Inactive, or vice versa, depending on what was selected in Step 1). After the update completes, reset the radio button and checkbox selections so the form is ready for the next round of updates.

This way, instead of updating customers one at a time, users can filter by status, multi-select as many customers as needed, and update them all in a single click.

entry.title: SAP Business Client 8.10
entry.content:
It's that time again - the new release will come tomorrow - July 16.

We are happy to launch SAP Business Client 8.10, together with SAP GUI for Windows 8.10 .

This release is fully downward compatible with former releases. The following new features have been implemented:

 

Horizon Theming:

Morning Horizon Theme
SAP Business Client 8.10 offers Horizon theming, including Morning Horizon, Evening Horizon and HC-variants. Additionally, Custom Theming for Horizon themes is available for On-Premise and Cloud systems.

Joule : 

Together with SAP GUI for Windows 8.10, the SAP Business Client allows users to use Joule for SAP GUI for Windows transactions, enabling Joule to access the current transaction and apply Frontend Actions.

Edge Browser Extensions: 
With release 8.10, the SAP Business Client is open to Edge browser extensions, allowing companies to enhance their SAP Business Client experience.

Admin Command Line Toolset: 
This toolset allows an administrator to modify and change data and client behaviour via command line and possibly batch.

Index, New Tab and Service Map page in WPF: 
Formerly HTML-based internal pages are now recreated in WPF for easier theming and control.

Tab Navigation via JS:
Via an extra API in the WindowExternal-object, it's possible to create and manage IB-navigated tabs in the Fiori Launchpad via Javascript code.

Quicklaunch improvements:
The SAP Business Client 8.10 provides an improved Quicklaunch including autocomplete, fuzzy search, share, zoom and add favorite functionalities.

Enhanced Back and Forward Navigation UI: 
Back and Forward navigation buttons are available and allow access to the list of past navigation targets.

Restore Mechanism: 
Like in a standalone web browser, in case of an unlikely crash or simply after logon, it's possible to restore the tabs from the former session.

System Selection Screen Improvements: 
Including advanced filtering across workspaces and non-alphabetic ordering.

And Many More ...

 

For full usage of SAP GUI for Windows within SAP Business Client 8.10, SAP GUI for Windows 8.10 is required.

entry.title: Re: SAP RDP - German EPR: Packaging in scope, drop shipme...
entry.content:
1. The scope of excluded / included packaging is described in the report documentation. Look at the page "Rules for reporting based on attribute value". Primary packaging without usage field or with usage Household will be included regardless of the customer role. Secondary and tertiary packaging is included, if the usage is Household or the customer is with certain roles (END_CONSUM, HORECA, HOSPITAL). Industrial packaging, reusable packaging and packaging with deposit is out of scope.

2. I think that Drop shipments are also in scope of the report. But the customer should check with their legal team if they are liable in the case of drop shipment or the supplier. Until RDP starts supporting the statistical GR records, what you describe is a feasible workaround.

3. We include only packaging with system participation required. Packaging which does not require system participation, but must be registered in LUCID can be covered with User-defined reports (like transport packaging). We already have at least one customer who created a User-defined report for its transport packaging obligations.
The below summary of obligations was generated by AI, so use it with caution.
If you ship packaging without system participation, you must still register in LUCID, but you're not obligated to report the quantities in LUCID. That's why RDP does not include it.

entry.title: Setting Up Expense Master Data in Commodity Expense Management
entry.content:
A Practical Guide for Business Users and Solution Designers

In Commodity Expense Management, setting up expense master data is not just a configuration activity—it’s a foundation for automation, accuracy, and financial transparency.

This blog walks through the structured approach to configuring expense master data, helping you understand how different applications work together to drive:

  • Intelligent expense determination
  • Automated accruals
  • Seamless financial postings

 

🧭 The Big Picture

For clarity and maintainability, expense master data is organized into four layers:

 

Basic

→             Define core expense objects

Foundation

→             Enable logic via fields & lookups

Mapping

→             Drive automation rules

Postings

→             Integrate with financial postings

Each layer builds on the previous one—so getting the design right upfront is critical.

 

1️⃣   Basic: Defining the Core Building Blocks

This is where you establish the what of expense management.

🔹Manage Expense Types

  • Logical grouping (e.g., Brokerage)
  • Enables business-semantic reporting
  • Supports multi-language descriptions
  • Where-used visibility

Lifecycle control:

  • An expense type can be de-activated to prevent any further usage if there are no active expense IDs.
  • An expense type can be deleted from the system if there are no expense IDs.

🔹Manage Expense IDs

Represents the actual expense.

Key design elements:

  • Mandatory link to Expense Type
  • Multi-language support
  • Where-used visibility

Lifecycle control:

  • An expense id can be de-activated to prevent any further usage if there are no active templates.
  • An expense id can be deleted from the system if there are no templates and transactional documents using it.

📌Firmed At vs Accrual Event

These two settings define when an expense becomes final and when it is financially recognized.

Firmed At

This setting defines when expense details (rate, service provider, payment terms, etc.) become final and unchangeable.

Options include:

  • Contract/Deal – Editable in Contract (Agri) / Deal (non-Agri), not beyond
  • Nomination – Editable up to Nomination stage
  • “-” – No restriction; editable across all documents

Accrual Event

This determines when an expense is considered incurred and should be accrued.

  • Contract Approved / Deal Published
  • Nomination Confirmed
  • Delivery Executed

👉Important Rule: Accrual Event ≥ Firmed At

🔹Manage Templates

Templates enable automation and defaulting of expenses during document creation (Contract or Deal).

A Template:

  • Contains a predefined list of expenses
  • Displays Expense Types, Firmed At, and Accrual Event indicators
  • Provides where-used visibility (via Lookups)

Lifecycle controls:

  • Deactivation (if not used in active lookups)
  • Deletion (if unused)

 

2️⃣   Foundation: Enabling Intelligent Logic

This layer defines the how behind determination.

🔹Manage Field Catalogs

Field Catalog define the input parameters used in Lookup Definitions.

Two field sources:

  • System – Predefined standard fields
  • User – Custom fields (namespace starting with Z)

Capabilities include:

  • Multi-language labels
  • Valid value definitions
  • Where-used tracking across Lookups
  • Fields can only be deleted if they are not referenced in any lookups

🔹Manage Look Up Definition

This is the most critical configuration step, as it governs how templates and expense details are determined. Two types of Lookups:

Template Determination

  • Identifies which Template to use
  • First field: Company Code (fixed)
  • Additional fields: configurable (System/User)

Expense Determination

  • Determines expense details (Service Provider, Rate, etc.)
  • First fields: Company Code and Expense ID (fixed)
  • Additional fields: configurable (System/User)

⚠️Once data is maintained → structure is locked

 

3️⃣  Mapping: Driving Automation

The Mapping layer operationalises the rules defined earlier to enable real-time determination.

🔹Manage Look Up Assignment

This step links Company Code → Lookup Definitions.

Key points:

  • One Template Lookup and one Expense Lookup per Company Code
  • Lookups can be reused across Company Codes
  • Missing assignment = no automation (manual entry of expenses required)

🔹Manage Template Determination

This app defines which Template gets selected based on input criteria.

Key characteristics:

  • Data maintained per Company Code
  • Dynamic structure based on Lookup Definition
  • Output field: Template ID
  • Allowed values: Values as defined in field Catalog and  “*” Any value

Now let us understand what this means with an example below

 

Commodity

Document Type

Incoterms

Template ID

SOYBEANS

3P Purchase

FOB

Template_SB_3P_FOB

SOYBEANS

3P Purchase

DDP

Template_SB_3P_DDP

SOYBEANS

3P Purchase

*

Template_SB_3P_ALL

So, when a contract (Agri) in this case is created the system looks out for exact match and if found then use the template for determining the list of expenses. If an exact match is not found, then the system looks out for a record by using generic search (from right to left) till it could determine a match to use the template for determining the list of expenses. If no match is found, then this results into no determination of expenses and therefore nothing is defaulted in contract.

🔹Manage Expense Determination

This defines how each Expense ID is populated with additional details such as:

  • Service Provider
  • Calculation Type
  • Rate / Amount
  • Currency, Units, UoM

Key capabilities:

  • Multi-provider support
  • Import/Export (Merge or Replace)
  • Upload logs with status tracking

Matching logic mirrors Template Determination:

  • Exact match → Use details
  • Generic match → Fallback logic
  • No match → Expense defaults without details

Below is a visual representation of how the top 3 layers stack up

 

4️⃣ Postings: Financial Integration

The Postings layer finalizes the setup by linking expenses to financial postings. This solution uses the Agency Business component for accruals and settlements.

🔹Manage Posting Assignments

Maps Expense ID to:

  • Pricing Condition Type
  • Accrual Document Type
  • Settlement Document Type
  • Offset Document Types

🔁Accrual & Settlement Behaviour

A key concept in Commodity Expense Management:

👉The system does NOT cancel postings
👉It creates offset entries

Accrual Posting (VBD)
                 ↓
Offset Accrual (Credit Memo)

Settlement Posting
                 ↓
Offset Settlement (Credit Memo)

⚠️Design Guideline:

  • Offset types must mirror original types
  • Must use Credit Memo category

 

🧠   Key Takeaways

  • Basic → Defines structure
  • Foundation → Enables logic
  • Mapping → Drives automation
  • Postings → Ensures financial accuracy

 

🚀   Final Thought

A well-designed expense master data setup enables:

✔️     Accurate planning

✔️     Intelligent automation

✔️     Real-time accruals

✔️     Clean financial postings

 

But more importantly—it creates a scalable and adaptable framework that can evolve with business complexity.

entry.title: How to Use our Free SAP Practice Systems in the Classroom
entry.content:
How to Use the Free SAP Practice Systems in the Classroom

If you teach with SAP in a university setting, you already know that hands-on experience is what makes the difference between students who understand SAP conceptually and students who can actually work with it. The free practice systems available through SAP Learning Hub, student edition offer exactly that kind of hands-on exposure — at no cost to your institution. But using them effectively in a classroom setting takes a bit of planning.

This post walks through what these systems are designed for, the constraints you will realistically run into when teaching a full class, and the strategies that can help you work around them.

 

What These Practice Systems Were Designed For

It helps to start with the original intent. These systems were built for individual, self-paced learners — someone who enrolls into a shared practice environment on their own schedule, completes a set of exercises over a limited time window, and exits before the next system refresh. That is a very different scenario from a professor coordinating 20, 40, or 60 students accessing the same system simultaneously during a scheduled class session.

Understanding that gap is the first step toward using these systems well in an academic context.

 

What Changes in a Classroom

When you move from a single self-directed learner to a synchronized group, a few constraints that would otherwise go unnoticed become significant.

Shared seat capacity is the most immediate one. The practice systems are shared across the entire pool of SAP Learning Hub subscribers — students, customers, and partners alike. There is no reservation mechanism for a class, which means there is no guarantee that all your students can log in at the same time. Two systems are notable exceptions, as they have been configured with enhanced capacity to better handle volume: the End-to-End Business Processes for SAP Business Suite system and the SAP Generative AI Developer Certification practice system. Other areas — SAP Analytics Cloud, for example — remain more challenging in an academic group setting.

Scheduled refresh and data reset is the second constraint to keep in mind. Every practice system runs on a defined availability window, and when that window closes, the system resets to its default state. Anything your students created or configured during their session is gone. The availability window is displayed before enrollment, so you can plan around it — but all student work must happen within that window, which requires your course timeline to align with the system calendar rather than the other way around.

Moving availability windows add another layer of complexity. A system stays dormant until someone triggers access, at which point the countdown begins. Window lengths typically range from 14 to 28 days depending on the solution. Students who enroll later in the cycle will have fewer days remaining before the next refresh, which means their experience is compressed compared to classmates who enrolled earlier.

 

Strategies for a Smooth Class Experience

None of these constraints are dealbreakers — they are just realities to design around. Here is what works.

Always have a Plan B. Because simultaneous access cannot be guaranteed at peak times, your class should not hinge on every student being logged in at the same moment. Build fallbacks into your lesson plan: recorded demos, instructor-led walkthroughs, or screenshots of key workflows can carry the session if access is constrained. Students who cannot get in during class can follow along visually and practice on their own time.

Shift some practice to extracurricular work. Rather than framing hands-on exercises as in-class activities that happen all at once, consider structuring them as optional assignments or extra credit that students complete asynchronously. Staggering work across a broader time window naturally distributes the demand on shared capacity and accommodates the variability in access windows.

Use flexible scheduling. Offering multiple submission windows or rolling start dates gives students room to work around access issues without falling behind. If a student hits a capacity limit during one session, they should have a clear path to complete the exercise when seats open up — without academic penalty for a constraint that is outside their control.

Build a broader curriculum around the practice systems, not on top of them. The most resilient course designs treat hands-on system access as one component among several. Combining practice time with our pre-made academic lesson plans, case studies, process diagrams, and discussion-based activities means learning continues even when system access is limited. Students get the conceptual grounding in parallel, so the live system time is used more efficiently when it is available.

 

How to Get Started

If you are new to SAP Learning Hub, student edition, SAP has put together onboarding videos and guidance at learning.sap.com/onboarding-videos. These walk through enrollment, system access, and the basics of navigating the available practice environments.

If you run into access issues or need support during a course, you can open a support case through the SAP Learning Help Center.

 

Bottom Line

The free SAP practice systems are a genuinely valuable resource for university instructors — they give students direct experience with enterprise software that would otherwise be inaccessible in an academic setting. But they are shared infrastructure designed for individual learners, and using them with a full class requires planning for the constraints that come with that.

Set realistic expectations around capacity, build flexibility into your course schedule, and have fallback materials ready. Do that, and these systems can anchor a hands-on learning experience your students will carry into their careers.

entry.title: Bulk/batch update for salesTeam in Opportunity Service REST API (Sales Cloud V2)
entry.content:
Hi all,

We're working with the Opportunity Service REST API on Sales Cloud Version 2:

POST /sap/c4c/api/v1/opportunity-service/opportunities/{opportunityId}/salesTeam{ "role": "Z101", "partyId": "019e8c48-adc4-7ff1-98d3-f2e46cfa9089"}

 

Right now we can only add one sales team member (role + partyId) per POST call. When we need to assign several roles/parties to the same Opportunity, this means one API call per entry.

I know the classic OData v1 API supports $batch with multipart changesets, but I can't find an equivalent for the V2 REST APIs (checked KBA 3508796 on query parameters — no mention of $batch there).

Questions:

  1. Has anyone found a way to submit multiple salesTeam entries in a single call for this API (e.g. posting an array to the collection endpoint)?
  2. Is there any bulk/batch mechanism at all for V2 REST APIs, or is one-by-one the only supported pattern right now?

    Thanks in advance

    Ruchita

entry.title: Building Durable AI Agents - Announcing the `cds-langgraph-persistence` Plugin
entry.content:
For the past year, I’ve been experimenting and building AI agents with LangChain/LangGraph inside the CAP Node.js runtime on SAP BTP. This has been less about “toy agents” and more about understanding what it actually takes to run agents reliably in SAP landscapes, next to real business systems and users.

When you look at AI agents as a software architecture (not just “LLM + tools”) and are planning to take it to production, you face the critical requirements: identity, authn/authz, multitenancy, persistence, event‑driven flows, scalability, observability, CI/CD, and so on. CAP already solves most of this for applications on BTP, which is why I’m increasingly convinced that “LangChain/LangGraph + CAP Node.js” is one of the best combinations for taking agents to production in SAP environments. I’ll share a more detailed take on that combo in a separate post.

In this post, let’s narrow the scope and focus on one foundational capability: durability — the ability for an agent to maintain state, survive failures, and resume multi‑step, long‑running tasks without losing its context. We’ll see how LangGraph’s checkpointing model works, and how a CAP‑native LangGraph/LangChain Checkpointer (via @mi8y/cds-langgraph-persistence) lets you bring durable agents to SAP BTP with minimal friction.

Thinking of agents as state machines

A useful mental model is to treat an AI agent as a "state machine." At any point in time, the agent has a “state”: the current conversation history, internal variables, partial results, tool outputs, and whatever else it needs to decide the next step in its workflow.

AI Agent as State Graph

As the agent works towards a goal, this "state" changes: new messages are added, tools are called, intermediate decisions are made. If you could take a snapshot of that state on every transition and store it in durable persistence, you would be able to:

  • Recover from crashes or infra failures by reloading the latest snapshot.
  • Resume long‑running workflows after delays (e.g. waiting for human approval).
  • Inspect what the agent did, step by step.
  • “Time‑travel” back to a previous checkpoint if needed. (useful for forking or replaying in case of an UI trying to catch up with a long-running agent)

This is exactly what LangGraph’s checkpointers are about: they provide a persistence layer that saves a snapshot of the graph’s state on each step and make it retrievable later, keyed by a thread or workflow id.

What are LangGraph Checkpoints?

LangGraph offers a checkpointing interface that you plug into your agent graph when you compile it. Each time the graph advances, the checkpointer records a snapshot of the state. These snapshots are stored somewhere (in‑memory or in a database), and referenced by a thread_id or similar key so you can resume or inspect a specific conversation. Please note that LangGraph, LangChain and DeepAgents all share the same checkpointing interface, so you can swap implementations without changing your agent logic.

Checkpointing in LangGraph

 

This unlocks

  • Conversational memory: the agent remembers previous turns without you manually managing history.
  • Fault tolerance: if the app restarts, you can reload the last checkpoint and continue.
  • Time travel and introspection: you can inspect or replay previous states.
  • Human‑in‑the‑loop: you can pause at a checkpoint, wait for human input, and resume from there.

The simplest way to get started is the in‑memory checkpointer that LangGraph ships with:

import { MemorySaver } from "@langchain/langgraph";

const checkpointer = new MemorySaver(); // in-memory; good for dev, loses state on restart

const agent = createAgent({
  checkpointer, // just pass the instance
  // other settings: model, tools, etc.
});

This is perfect for local experimentation and debugging, but it only keeps checkpoints in memory. Once your CAP app restarts or scales, those checkpoints are gone. For SAP BTP and CAP, you need something that speaks to your actual persistence layer and fits into your multi‑tenant, production setup.

Why a CAP/CDS‑native checkpointer?

Out of the box, LangGraph/LangChain provide checkpointer implementations for stores like SQLite (good for local dev), Postgres, Redis and other generic backends for production. In a typical non‑SAP BTP environment, that’s often enough.

On SAP BTP with CAP and SAP HANA Cloud, things are slightly more nuanced:

  • You might be using HDI containers per tenant, with separate schemas.
  • You might just have a single HANA database
  • You need agents to run locally for dev and in CI pipelines for agent evals without heavyweight setup.
  • You don’t want to bolt on an entirely different persistence stack just for agent state, bypassing CAP’s data model and lifecycle.

To solve this, I'm announcing an NPM package - @mi8y/cds-langgraph-persistence. This combines two roles:

  • A CDS plugin that wires the required persistence entities into your CAP data model.
  • A LangGraph/LangChain checkpointer adapter that uses those entities to store and retrieve checkpoints.

This means:

  • You stay within CAP’s multitenancy and lifecycle model.
  • You don’t need to manually create or deploy tables for checkpoints.
  • The same code works locally, in CI, and on SAP BTP, as long as CAP can talk to the configured database.

The code is opensource and MIT licensed. The package is designed to work with CAP v9 and the latest v10, and acts as a drop‑in replacement for any checkpointer you might already be using.

Here’s what swapping looks like at the agent level:

import { CdsCheckpointSaver } from "@mi8y/cds-langgraph-persistence";

- const checkpointer = new MemorySaver(); // any non-CAP checkpointer
+ const checkpointer = new CdsCheckpointSaver({
+   name: "my-agent",
+ });

const agent = createAgent({
  checkpointer,
  // ...
});

Internally, the CDS plugin injects the necessary entities into your model, while CAP takes care of tenant routing, schema management, and lifecycle. You get durability and multitenancy “for free”, without custom plumbing.

Multi‑tenancy and lifecycle, the CAP way

Because the plugin adds entities directly into your CDS model, CAP treats checkpoint data like any other CAP‑managed entity:

  • Multitenancy: if your CAP app is configured for multitenancy, CAP routes checkpoint reads/writes to the correct tenant container based on the request context. You don’t have to manually add tenant IDs to agent state or build your own sharding logic.
  • Lifecycle: you don’t need to deploy checkpoint tables separately. CAP’s usual deployment flow handles entity lifecycle, whether you’re using SQLite, HANA, Postgres or any custom DB adapter during development and production.

Getting started in a CAP project

Let’s walk through a minimal example of wiring a LangGraph agent with CAP and the CDS checkpointer.

1. Install the package

In your CAP Node.js project:

npm install @mi8y/cds-langgraph-persistence

2. Define a simple agent service

Expose a REST action that will drive the agent, passing in a threadId and the latest user message:

@protocol: 'rest'
service AgentService {
  action invoke(threadId: String, content: String) returns String;
}

3. Add a custom CDS handler with the checkpointer

In your service implementation, initialize the checkpointer and agent once, then use them inside the action handler:

import cds from "@sap/cds";
import { CdsCheckpointSaver } from "@mi8y/cds-langgraph-persistence";
import { createAgent } from "./agent"; // your LangGraph agent factory

export class AgentService extends cds.ApplicationService {
  init() {
    // Initialize a CAP-aware checkpointer for an agent named "my-agent"
    const checkpointSaver = new CdsCheckpointSaver({ name: "my-agent" });

    const agent = createAgent({
      checkpointer: checkpointSaver,
      // other configuration: model, system prompt, tools, etc.
    });

    this.on("invoke", async (req) => {
      const { threadId, content } = req.data;

      // Invoke the agent with just the latest user message
      const res = await agent.invoke(
        { messages: [{ role: "user", content }] },
        {
          configurable: {
            // Use a unique threadId per user to isolate conversation threads
            thread_id: `${threadId}-${req.user.id}`,
          },
        }
      );

      // Return the last message content from the agent
      return res.messages[res.messages.length - 1].content;
    });

    return super.init();
  }
}

A few important points:

  • You only pass the latest user message into the agent. You don’t manually maintain full history in your service code. Checkpointer handles that for you.
  • The thread_id inside configurable is the key that LangGraph uses to group checkpoints. Combining threadId with req.user.id gives you per‑user, per‑thread isolation.
  • The checkpointer persists each step of the graph for that thread_id into CAP’s persistence. When the next request comes in with the same threadId, the agent resumes with full context.

4. Invoke the agent and see durability in action

With the service deployed, you can start a conversation:

POST /rest/agent/invoke
Content-Type: application/json

{
  "threadId": "test-thread-1",
  "content": "Give me the list of all the books in the catalog."
}

Then, continue the conversation with the same threadId:

POST /rest/agent/invoke
Content-Type: application/json

{
  "threadId": "test-thread-1",
  "content": "What questions have I asked you so far?"
}

Even though you only send the latest user message, the agent can recall the previous turns because the checkpointer has been saving and loading the full state behind the scenes.

To test isolation, try a different threadId:

POST /rest/agent/invoke
Content-Type: application/json

{
  "threadId": "test-thread-2",
  "content": "What questions have I asked you so far?"
}

This should behave like a fresh conversation, since it uses a different thread and therefore a different checkpoint stream.

If you want to see a complete example, including the agent definition and tools, you can look at the LangChain example in the GitHub repo: examples/langchain-cds-persistence.

Practical best practices for durable agents on CAP

Once you have durability, a few patterns help keep things sane in production:

  • Use meaningful threadId values
    Treat threadId as a business‑level concept: a conversation, a workflow instance, a support ticket, etc. Don’t just generate random IDs with no semantic meaning.

  • Let CAP own multitenancy
    The package injects entities into your CDS model, and CAP handles tenant routing. Avoid building custom tenant switches inside your agent logic; rely on CAP’s standard patterns instead.

  • Plan checkpoint lifecycle
    Checkpoints will accumulate over time. You should periodically clean up old threads that are no longer active. For example:

    const staleThreads = await tx.run(
  SELECT.from(Checkpoints)
    .columns("graphName", "namespace", "threadId", "max(createdAt) as lastActivity")
    .groupBy("graphName", "namespace", "threadId")
    .having("max(createdAt) <", cutoffTime)
);

    You can then delete checkpoints for those stale threads, or archive them depending on your compliance needs.

  • Expose entities for debugging (optionally)
    For troubleshooting and introspection, you can project the plugin’s entities into your own service:

    using plugin.langgraph.persistence as langgraph from '@mi8y/cds-langgraph-persistence';

service CheckpointViewerService {
  entity Checkpoints as projection on langgraph.Checkpoints; // view all checkpoints
}

    This is handy during development or incident analysis, but you don’t need to expose such viewers in production if you have other observability tooling.

Durability is just one piece of building production‑ready agents. You still need observability, evals, guardrails, and human‑in‑the‑loop patterns that fit your SAP landscape. The goal of @mi8y/cds-langgraph-persistence is to make the durability part boring and reliable. In the future blogs, I'll cover other aspects of taking agents to production.

  • GitHub repo: https://github.com/mi8y/cds-langgraph-persistence
  • NPM package: @mi8y/cds-langgraph-persistence

Disclaimer: This article was written by me, but the narrative refined and type-checked by AI.
